coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots sensitized 2D/2D carbon nitride nanosheets/bismuth tungstate for visible light photocatalytic degradation norfloxacin
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Xinghao
Yang, Zhaoguang
Yang, Ying
Li, Haipu
coredata.dc:description: A novel carbon quantum dots (CQDs) sensitized 2D/2D carbon nitride nanosheets and bismuth tungstate composite (CQD-CNs/BWO) was successfully prepared via the facile hydrothermal method and used for the photocatalytic degradation of norfloxacin (NOR). During 120 min irradiation test, CQD-CNs/BWO exhibited 9 and 1.76 times higher photocatalytic activity than CNs and BWO, respectively. CQDs and constructed 2D/2D structure could not only improve the light harvesting but also promote the generation and separation of electron-holes. The existing inorganic ions in solution (e.g. bicarbonate ions, chlorine ions, and sulfate ions) could inhibit NOR degradation. Based on the electron spin resonance and free radicals inhibition tests, the holes and superoxide radicals rather than hydroxyl radicals were the main reactive species. The intermediates and possible pathways were proposed, and the antibacterial activity of the treated solution after the reaction was evaluated via bacteriostatic tests. The prepared composite material with high photocatalytic activity and stability is potentially effective for the degradation of antibiotics in wastewater.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel carbon quantum dots (CQDs) sensitized 2D/2D carbon nitride nanosheets and bismuth tungstate composite (CQD-CNs/BWO) was successfully prepared via the facile hydrothermal method and used for the photocatalytic degradation of norfloxacin (NOR). During 120 min irradiation test, CQD-CNs/BWO exhibited 9 and 1.76 times higher photocatalytic activity than CNs and BWO, respectively. CQDs and constructed 2D/2D structure could not only improve the light harvesting but also promote the generation and separation of electron-holes. The existing inorganic ions in solution (e.g. bicarbonate ions, chlorine ions, and sulfate ions) could inhibit NOR degradation. Based on the electron spin resonance and free radicals inhibition tests, the holes and superoxide radicals rather than hydroxyl radicals were the main reactive species. The intermediates and possible pathways were proposed, and the antibacterial activity of the treated solution after the reaction was evaluated via bacteriostatic tests. The prepared composite material with high photocatalytic activity and stability is potentially effective for the degradation of antibiotics in wastewater.

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental issues with organic contaminants, including antibiotics, dyes, and oil refining in the water environment, have become a great challenge to the sustainable development of society. Various methods including ion exchange and adsorption (Naushad et al., 2016; Sharma et al., 2018) have been proven to be effective in removing contaminants from aqueous solution. These methods can separate and enrich pollutants but are difficult to degrade them. Photo-catalysis as a clean production technology is the simplest way to solve this problem. In order to improve photocatalytic activity and light utilization, researchers are committed to the development of novel photo-catalysts such as carbon-based material (Sr/Ce/activated carbon bimetallic nanocomposite) and magnetic material (Fe3O4/ZnO/Si3N4 nanocomposite) (Sharma et al., 2019a, 2020), showing high efficiency in degrading pollutants in solution. Therefore, a highly efficient photo-catalyst should possess the following characteristics: rapid separation of electron-holes, inhibition of electron-hole recombination, and high utilization efficiency of light (Spasiano et al., 2015; Tang et al., 2014).

Bismuth tungstate (Bi2WO6, denoted as BWO), as a narrow bandgap photo-catalyst (Sheng et al., 2014) with a unique layered structure, i.e., alternating accumulation of WO6 − octahedral nano-sheets and (Bi2O2)2+ layers, brings about some superiorities, including thermal and chemical stability, and excellent catalytic activity (Li et al., 2014; Zhu et al., 2017). To improve the structure and function of BWO, preparation methods including solid-state, sol-gel, and hydrothermal reactions have been proposed (Liu and Yu, 2008; Zhang et al., 2010a, 2013). The prepared Bi2WO6 has been used to remove contaminants. For example, Zhang et al. (2010b) used Bi2WO6 to degrade RhB and phenol with degradation ratios of 11% and 15% after 75 min and 180 min visible light irradiation, respectively. Using Bi2WO6 alone has some fatal shortcomings, such as shorter absorption wavelength (less than 450 nm), faster recombination velocity of generated electron-holes, and lower quantum efficiency, which limit its practical application (Ju et al., 2016; Low et al., 2014; Zhou et al., 2014). To overcome these drawbacks, broadening the absorption band, promoting electron transfer, and suppressing electron-hole recombination are beneficial to photocatalytic activity. At present, modifying metal or non-metal chemical deposition and constructing heterogeneous structures are common methods (Wen et al., 2016; Zhao et al., 2019). It is a promising technology to construct a specific interface between well-matched different semiconductor materials with different band gaps. And the constructed structure can effectively promote the transformation of electrons and the separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs.

Among the tremendous photo-catalysts, carbon nitride (g-C3N4) with light catalytic activity, thermal, and chemical stability, is a suitable candidate for binding to Bi2WO6. The g-C3N4 with a narrow bandgap was used to split water to produce hydrogen under visible light irradiation (Wang et al., 2009). After then, g-C3N4 is considered as an aussichtsreich catalyst to produce hydrogen and remove contaminants. Moreover, g-C3N4 can cause a red shift in the wavelength of light absorption and improve light utilization efficiency (Sahu et al., 2021). Therefore, combination with other semiconductor materials can not only enhance the absorption of visible light but also enhance the semiconductor photochemical stability. The hetero-structure composite of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 has been successively prepared and its photocatalytic activity is better than pure g-C3N4 or Bi2WO6 (Lian et al., 2021). The conduction band (CB) of g-C3N4 is smaller than that of Bi2WO6 and the valence band (VB) of Bi2WO6 is higher than that of g-C3N4. Thus, the photo-generated electrons transfer from the CB of g-C3N4 to the CB of Bi2WO6, and the photo-generated holes transfer from the VB of Bi2WO6 to the VB of g-C3N4 (Xiong et al., 2016). The fabricated g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 composite can effectively promote the separation and transformation of electron-hole pairs and inhibit the recombination of electron-hole pairs. However, Bi2WO6 can only absorb light with wavelength less than 450 nm, which leads to generate less electrons (Gao et al., 2018). Therefore, there are still a few promising developments for the g-C3N4/Bi2WO6.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as new carbon material have been widely used as the effective photo-catalyst design component, because of their excellent characteristics including low cost, abundant surface functional groups, up-conversion photoluminescence behavior, low toxicity, and good photo-generated electron transfer performance (Zhao et al., 2015; Zuo et al., 2016). Many semiconductor materials (e.g., Bi2WO6, g-C3N4, and TiO2) have been modified with CQDs to improve photocatalytic activity (Di et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2016b; Xu et al., 2019). TiO2 can not be excited by visible light irradiation, and the photocatalytic activity of Bi2WO6 and g-C3N4 are weak under the visible-light region. The photocatalytic activities are significantly improved after the materials are modified with CQDs, which is attributed to the light-induced conversion behavior, and excellent electron transfer and/or storage capabilities of CQDs. However, the relationship between structure and photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 and CQDs is still not well understood and the roles of CQDs in the prepared materials still need further study. Because of the advantages above mentioned of g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 and CQDs, if CQDs successfully combine with g-C3N4/Bi2WO6, the special structure with higher photocatalytic activity can likely be realized.

In this work, the CQD-C3N4/Bi2WO6 (CQD-CNs/BWO) composite was prepared via a facile hydrothermal method. The photocatalytic performance of the prepared material was evaluated by the degradation of norfloxacin (NOR) under visible light irradiation. The generated reactive species were investigated using the trapping experiment and were further examined by electron spin resonance (ESR). Considering that, water quality parameters, such as ion types, may reduce the photocatalytic performance of CQD-CNs/BWO, we added the anions that might negatively affect the performance (e.g. Cl−, HCO3 −, or SO4 2 −) to the photocatalytic system to investigate their effects on NOR degradation. Based on this, the mechanisms for improving visible light absorption and enhancing photo-generated electron-hole separation and transfer efficiency were proposed. At last, the pathway of NOR degradation was proposed and the antibacterial activity of solution after the reaction was evaluated.

NOR (99.9%), bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), sodium tungstate dihydrate (Na2WO4·2H2O), 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO), 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidinooxy (TEMPO), cetyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB), citric acid (CA), and melamine were supplied by Aladdin Industrial Corporation. Ammonium oxalate (AO), p-benzoquinone (BQ), and tert-butanol (TBA) were gotten from Shanghai Macklin Industrial Corporation. The acetonitrile and formic acid (FA) (High performance liquid chromatography grade) were purchased by Tedia Industrial Corporation. And the analytic reagents were used without further purification. All the experiments use ultrapure water (UP-water, electro-conductibility, 0.1–0.055 μs/cm).
The procedure for preparing the composite material was shown in Fig. S1 in Supplementary Materials.

Bulk g-C3N4 was obtained by pyrolysis and polymerization of melamine at 550 °C at a heating rate of 3 °C/min under an N2 atmosphere (Zheng et al., 2015). The above g-C3N4 was dissolved into the methyl alcohol and ultrasonicated for 24 h to give g-C3N4 nanosheets.

The 2D/2D CNs/BWO was prepared by the hydrothermal method (Wang et al., 2018b). A certain amount of CNs, 1.649 g Na2WO4·2H2O, 4.85 g Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, and 50 mg CTAB were added into 80 mL UP-water. After stirring, the suspension was placed into the reactor to undergo hydrothermal reaction at the condition of 200 °C for 24 h. The solids were cleaned and purified by washing with UP-water and ethanol three times, respectively, and then dried at 80 °C for 12 h. Bi2WO6 was prepared via the above preparation process without CNs.

CQDs were prepared by the upward transformation with heating CA, which was the same as our previous research (Liu et al., 2021). The pH of the 0.2 mol/L CA solution was adjusted to 7 by NH3·H2O. After stirring for 30 min, the solution was placed in the reactor to undergo hydrothermal reaction at the condition of 160 °C for 4 h. The obtained solution was separated and purified via a dialysis bag to form the solution containing CQDs. Finally, the solution was freeze-dried to generate CQDs.

CQD-CNs/BWO was synthesized by a facile process (Fig. S1). Different amounts of CQDs were added into the suspensions each containing 200 mg 2D/2D CNs/BWO, and the mixtures were stirred for 12 h and dried at 80 °C overnight. At last, the theoretical contents of CQDs in composite materials were 0%, 0.5%, 1%, and 2%, respectively. The details on characterization were provided in Supplementary Materials, Text S1.

The photocatalytic performances of materials were assessed by degradation of NOR under the 500 W Xenon lamp irradiation. The photo-catalyst (50 mg) was added into model wastewater (100 mL, 15 mg/L NOR). Before visible light irradiation, the suspension was stirred for 30 min to reach adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Then, a suspension of 2 mL was collected at the preset times and filtered with 0.22 μm membrane filters for analysis. The analytical methods were shown in Text S2. All measurement data were the average of triplicate determinations. The whole photocatalytic system was shown in our previous research (Liu et al., 2021).
The EIS was measured by a CHI604 electrochemical workstation using a standard three-electrode cell. And the details on the method for preparing the working electrode were shown in Text S3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bacteriostatic activity of the treated solution
ce:section.ce:para: The bacteriostatic activities of NOR and its intermediates were assessed by the inhibition zone method. The operating steps were shown in Text S4.

The size and morphology of the prepared samples (CQDs, CNs/BWO, and CQD-CNs/BWO) were investigated by HRTEM (High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope). As shown in Fig. 1 (a), the CQD-CNs/BWO sample was nanosheets-like structure, and small particles were evenly distributed across the surface of the sheet structure. As shown in Fig. 1(b), the lattice spacing was about 0.315 nm, which was in accordance with the BWO nanosheet (113) plane (Wang et al., 2016), and the lattice spacing of 0.336 nm corresponded to the lattice plane (002) of CNs (Wu et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2014). The above conclusion proved that the sample was composed of CNs and BWO. In Fig. S2, the CNs/BWO sample possessed a sheet structure and there were no small particles on its surface (Mao et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2007). As shown in the morphology of the pure CQDs sample (Fig. S3), they were evenly distributed with an average diameter of approximately 10.25 nm (Liu et al., 2019b). And its average diameter was similar to the particle diameter on CNs/BWO samples, indicating that the small particles were CQDs. In addition, as revealed by the SEM (Scanning Electron Microscope) images (Fig. 1(c)), the CQD-CNs/BWO sample was lamellar in structure, and CNs were on the surface of BWO, which was consistent with the conclusion of HRTEM. According to the EDS (Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy) analysis, elements including C, O, N, W, and Bi were present in the prepared material.

The crystal feature of prepared samples was investigated by XRD (X-Ray Diffraction). In Fig. 2 (a), the peaks of the samples were located at about 28.3°, 32.8°, 47.04°, 55.8°, 58.5°, and 68.8°, which were attributed to the (113), (200), (202), (331), (262), and (400) planes of Bi2WO3 (JCPDS, No. 39–0256) (Wu et al., 2007; Zheng et al., 2015). The peaks at 12.8° and 27.6° were assigned to the (100) and (002) planes of g-C3N4, which correspond to the triazine repeat units and inter-planar stacking of g-C3N4, respectively (Wang et al., 2018b; Zhang et al., 2014). When CNs were combined with BWO, there was almost no low-angle impurity peak, indicating that the small molecules of CNs could be reassembled on the surface of BWO (Wang et al., 2018b). In addition, the characteristic peak of CNs at 27.6° was close to the peak of BWO at 28.3°, and it might be sheltered by the latter (Xu et al., 2013). As expected, no typical diffraction peaks of CQDs appeared in the sample of CQD-CNs/BWO due to the amorphousness of CQDs in the study (Liu et al., 2021), which could also explain why the interplanar spacing of CQDs could not be observed in HRTEM.

The surface functional groups of prepared materials were detected by FTIR (Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy), as shown in Fig. 2(b). As for CNs, the peaks in the range of 1000–1800 cm−1 and characteristic peaks located at about 807 cm−1 were attributed to vibration of C–N (CN) and breathing mode of s-triazine units, respectively (Li et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2018). For BWO, The absorption peaks at 702 and 813 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of WO and BiO bonds, respectively. In addition, the peak at 1380 cm−1 could be assigned to W–O–W stretching modes (Di et al., 2015). The spectra of CNs/BWO and CQD-CNs/BWO samples contained all characteristic peaks of CNs and BWO. When CNs/BWO was modified with CQDs, a new peak (1460 cm−1) appeared, which was attributed to –COO− and demonstrated the presence of CQDs (Li et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2019). Furthermore, the intensity of the peak increased as the content of CQDs increasing.

The C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, W 4f, and Bi 4f in survey spectra correlated with chemical binding energies of 288.2, 529.8, 398.8, 35.2, and 164.7 eV, respectively, which was in keeping with the result of EDS elemental analysis (Fig. S4). And element contents were listed in Table S1. In the sample of CNs/BWO modified with CQDs, the atomic C content increased from 14.42% to 20.23%, indicating that carbon material was successfully loaded onto the surface of CNs/BWO. Fig. 2(c–f) and Fig. S5 showed the different elements' chemical bonding states for CQD-CNs/BWO and CNs/BWO, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2(c), the C 1s of CQD-CNs/BWO could be de-convolved into three peaks including graphite phase carbon, C–N, and –COO−, which were located at 284.88, 286.57, and 288.5 eV, respectively (Zhang et al., 2019). And for CNs/BWO (Fig. S5(a)), the C 1s could only be assigned to graphite phase carbon and C–N, which were derived from the prepared precursor of CNs (Li et al., 2016), indicating that the functional group of –COO− came from CQDs. The conclusion was consistent with the FTIR analysis. For the O 1s of CQD-CNs/BWO (Fig. 2(d)), the peaks located at 530.4, 532.0, and 533.2 eV were ascribed to the –OH/O2, CO, and C–O, respectively; however, for CNs/BWO (Fig. S5(b)), there was only one peak of –OH/O2. These phenomena could be explained that –OH/O2 came from the adsorption H2O/O2, while CO and C–O from CQDs. As shown in Fig. 2(e), the peaks (35.58 and 37.72 eV) were ascribed to the vibration of W 4f (Di et al., 2016; Li et al., 2015a). And in Fig. 2(f), the peaks located at 159.33 and 146.64 eV were assigned to the Bi 4f 7/2 and Bi 4f 5/2, respectively (Xia and Lo, 2016; Zhao et al., 2018). The above discussion confirmed the existence of CQDs in CQD-CNs/BWO.

In Fig. 3 (a), the degradation ratio without photocatalytic material was negligible after 120 min visible light irradiation. The degradation ratios with CNs and BWO were limited (about 9.8% and 33.1%), while almost 47.5% degradation ratios with CNs/BWO occurred under the same conditions. Excitingly, CQD-CNs/BWO possessed the best degradation performance (about 67.7%) compared to single CNs, BWO, and CNs/BWO. Also, CNs/BWO was decorated by different CQDs contents (0.5%, 1%, and 2%). The 1% CQD-CNs/BWO had the highest degradation ratio with a reaction rate constant of 0.00766 min−1, compared to 0.00587 and 0.00592 min−1 for 0.5% and 2% CQD-CNs/BWO, respectively. When CQDs contents were more than 1%, further increasing the CQDs contents would lead to a decrease in the photocatalytic activity of CQD-CNs/BWO (Hong et al., 2016). Although CQDs as the electron acceptors could accelerate the photo-induced electron transfer, too many CQDs on CNs/BWO could reduce light utilization. This finding was consistent with that in related studies (Di et al., 2015, 2016). Therefore, 1% CQD-CNs/BWO was chosen as the optimum dosage of photo-catalyst in the following experiments.

Importantly, the photocatalytic performance of CQD-CNs/BWO for degradation of NOR was compared with other materials, as listed in Table S2. Among the reported materials, the Z-scheme AgBr/LaNiO3/g-C3N4 possessed an effective degradation of NOR (degradation ratio of 92%) in 240 min under 500 W Xenon lamp irradiation (Zhang et al., 2020a). The degradation ratios of NOR using ZnS-RGO, rGO/Bi4O5Br2, Mg/Bi2WO6 were about 92% (240 min), 81% (60 min), and 89% (150 min), respectively (Bai et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2021; Zhu et al., 2020). Compared to these pioneer works, although the novel CQD-CNs/BWO showed low competitiveness in efficiency (67.6%, 120 min), it is competitive in terms of raw material price and energy consumption, and might have potential application in environmental purification.

As shown in Fig. 3(c), the various water sources including tap-water, river water, and UP-water had different effects for NOR degradation, and the degradation ratios were approximately 67.6%, 36.1%, and 26.1% NOR in UP-water, tap water, and river water, respectively. The lowest degradation ratio was obtained in river water. The water quality parameters of tap water and river water were shown in Table S3. The soluble natural organic matters could be represented by the TOC of the solution and react competitively with the generated reactive species (Li et al., 2015b). Hence, the existing natural organic matters in solution were the main competitors for photo-generated reactive species (Wang et al., 2019). Furthermore, the degradation ratio could increase with increasing irradiation time, indicating that CQD-CNs/BWO as an effective photo-catalyst possessed promising practicality for water purification.

Inorganic ions including bicarbonate ions (HCO3 −), chlorine (Cl−), and sulfate (SO4 2−) are present in natural water in the form of salts. Therefore, the photocatalytic system containing NaHCO3, NaCl, and Na2SO4, respectively, was used to explore the effect on the degradation of NOR. As shown in Fig. 3(d), the degradation ratios in the presence of 5 mmol/L inorganic ions including HCO3 −, SO4 2− and Cl−, respectively, were followed by 24.4%, 40%, and 56.9% (HCO3 − < SO4 2− < Cl−). Cl− had a slightly negative effect on degradation of NOR, which was caused by the competitive adsorption between Cl− and NOR (Jiang et al., 2019). HCO3 − and SO4 2− had an obvious negative effect on degradation of NOR. As known, HCO3 − was the scavenger of ·OH and h+, and SO4 2− was the scavenger of ·OH. The reactions could follow the following equations (Eqs. (1)–(3)) (Zhou et al., 2010):

SO4 2− and HCO3 − could react with the reactive species to produce ·SO4 − and ·CO3 −. The latter possessed some oxidation capacity, which was weaker than those of ·OH and h+ (Yan and Yang, 2011). Therefore, the degradation of NOR often took a long time in the presence of ·SO4 − and ·CO3 −.

To detect the roles of reactive species including ·OH, ·O2 −, and h+, for NOR degradation, AO (10 mmol/L, h+ inhibitor), TBA (10 mmol/L, ·OH inhibitor), and BQ (5 mmol/L, ·O2 − inhibitor) were added into the reaction solution, respectively (Dhiman et al., 2017). The solution of BQ is colored, and the color of high concentration of BQ can interfere with the experiment, but low concentration of BQ hardly did. In Fig. 4 (a) and Fig. S6, the degradation ratio decreased by 40.82% (AO), 25.08% (BQ), and 6.82% (TBA), respectively. And the corresponding inhibition rates were 60.8% (AO over h+), 37.08% (BQ over ·O2 −), and 10.09% (TBA over ·OH), respectively. Accordingly, the total inhibition rate (AO, BQ, and TBA), or in other words, the total contribution rate (h+, ·O2 −, and ·OH), could be determined as 107.57%. The reason for this value slightly exceeding 100% was due to the fact that there were small overlaps in the effect of the inhibitors on reactive species (He et al., 2016). The above result suggested that these three reactive species played key roles in the degradation process compared to other possible reactive species such as singlet oxygen (1O2) or hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). This finding was consistent with the observation by Zhang et al. (2020b), who found the addition of the NaN3 (1O2, inhibitor) into the photocatalytic system resulted in only a negligible inhibition rate of 0.099%. They also noted that the yield of H2O2 was as low as 2.0 μmol after 5 h of reaction.

To confirm the role of the photo-generated reactive species, the spin-trapping ESR technique was employed. The reactive species,·O2 −, h+ and ·OH, were detected in the solutions of methyl alcohol, UP-water, and UP-water, respectively, at the condition of darkness (3 min) and visible light irradiation (5 min). As shown in Fig. 4(b–d), the ESR signals for DMPO-·O2 −, TEMPO-h+, and DMPO-·OH were observed, indicating that the photocatalytic system could generate ·O2 −, h+, and ·OH (Sharma et al., 2019b). In addition, the ESR signals of DMPO-·O2 − and TEMPO-h+ were remarkably collected, and the signals of DMPO-·O2 − were getting much stronger with increasing visible light irradiation time (Guo et al., 2021), while signals of TEMPO-h+ were getting much weaker with increasing visible light irradiation time, which could be interpreted as that h+ could contribute to the enhancement of photocatalytic performance (Wu et al., 2021). However, the signals of DMPO-·OH were not obvious under visible light irradiation and the intensity of signals did not increase significantly with increasing light time. The results of ESR made clear that ·O2 − and h+ were generated in the photocatalytic process; however, very little ·OH was generated, which were accordant with the results of the free radical capture experiment.

The photocatalytic degradation experiments indicated that CQD-CNs/BWO possessed good photocatalytic performance for NOR degradation under visible light irradiation. Based on the above researches, the mechanism of NOR degradation by CQD-CNs/BWO was proposed, which could provide theoretical support for further enhancement of the photocatalytic activity of new composite materials.

Ordinarily, photocatalytic properties of materials are closely related to light absorption and quantum efficiency, which are mainly determined by the efficiency of generation, separation, and transfer of the photo-generated electron-holes (Wu et al., 2016). The UV–vis DSR (UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra) could reflect the material's ability to absorb light (Fig. 5 (a)). CNs/BWO with heterojunction structure could broaden the absorption band to 470 nm compared to 450 nm for single BWO, which improved the light utilization and further promoted more photo-induced electron-holes (Le et al., 2019). When CNs/BWO with heterojunction structure was modified with different content CQDs (0.5%, 1%, and 2%), their light absorption band could be extended to 489, 588, and 502 nm, respectively. The best absorption band and the highest photocatalytic activity were obtained when CNs/BWO was loaded with 1% CQDs. The decrease in performance with increasing dosage of CQDs more than 1% was probably due to the opacity and light scattering of CQDs that reduced the absorption of the incident light as well as the reduced catalytic active sites (Yu et al., 2014). According to the calculated Tauc's plot (Fig. 5(b)), the band gaps of CNs, BWO, CNs/BWO, and 1% CQD-CNs/BWO were about 2.9, 2.8, 2.7, and 2.55 eV, respectively. And the CB and VB edge could be obtained via the empirical equations (Eqs. (4) and (5)) (Dhiman et al., 2017). The ECB and EVB for CNs were −1.31 and 1.59 eV, respectively, while those were 0.46 and 3.26 eV for BWO.

where X is the electronegativity of material, and the values are 4.64 and 6.36 eV for CNs and BWO, respectively. The E 0 is the free electron energy (4.5 eV).

The PL spectra could explicate the recombination ability of photo-generated electron-holes. The stronger the intensity of PL was, the faster the photo-generated electron-hole recombination was (Huang et al., 2017a; Li et al., 2021). As shown in Fig. 5(c), the intensity of PL of CNs/BWO was weaker than those of CNs and BWO, indicating that CNs/BWO with heterojunction structure could effectively inhibit the recombination of electrons and holes. When CNs/BWO was modified by CQDs, the intensities of PL continued to weaken. In addition, the PL peaks of CQD-CNs/BWO showed a little red shift, which was consistent with other reports (Liu et al., 2016a). The red shift was possible due to the narrowing of band gaps and the transfer of electrons from CNs/BWO to CQDs (Wang et al., 2017a). The results proved that CQDs as an electron conduction bridge could promote the separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs and inhibit the recombination of them (Liu et al., 2019b; Zhao and Chung, 2018). The EIS could describe the interfacial charge transfer characteristics (Guo et al., 2021). As usual, the diameter of the curve was in keeping with the charge transferability, with a small diameter indicating that the material possesses a lower resistance and faster electron transfer (Leelavathi et al., 2014). As shown in Fig. 5(d), CNs/BWO with 2D/2D heterogeneous structures possessed a smaller diameter compared to those of CNs or BWO, showing that the heterogeneous structure could promote the transfer of electrons (Wang et al., 2018a). The diameters of EIS continued to decrease with the addition of CQDs, and the smallest diameter was obtained when 1% of CQDs was loaded on CNs/BWO, indicating that the 1% CQD-CNs/BWO possessed the lowest charge transfer resistance. The phenomenon was attributed to that the CQDs and 2D/2D structure could promote the photo-generated electron transfer (Wang et al., 2018a; Zhang et al., 2019). Based on the UV–vis DSR, PL and EIS analyses, CQDs and CNs/BWO with heterojunction structure could not only broaden the absorption band, but also promote electron-hole transfer and inhibit electron-hole recombination, which could explain that the best photocatalytic performance was obtained at the 1% CQD-CNs/BWO. As known, the BWO and CNs were excited to generate corresponding electron-holes (Wang et al., 2017b). According to calculation, the CB of BWO was lower than that of CNs, the photo-generated electrons could be injected easily from CNs into the BWO. CQDs, as an electron conduction bridge, were good for the conversion of electrons from CNs to BWO. And the VB of BWO was lower than that of CNs, the photo-generated holes could be transferred from BWO into the CNs (Cui et al., 2012) (Eqs. (6)–(8), and Fig. 6 ). Because of the excellent characteristics including wide absorption band, rapid transfer of electrons, and inhibited electron-hole recombination of CQD-CNs/BWO, the photocatalytic system could generate more electron-hole pairs. The electrons, as good reductants in CQD-CNs/BWO could capture O2 on the surface and reduce it to ·O2 − (Liu et al., 2019a), which was the main reactive species to react with pollutants (Fig. 6) (Yang et al., 2019). The photo-generated holes could also react with H2O or –OH to produce ·OH, leading to a constant stream of the surface ·OH groups (Ge et al., 2011). The formation of ·O2 −., ·OH, and h+ could react with target pollutants. The processes were described by Eqs. 9–11 (Yi et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The proposed intermediates and degradation pathways
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic mechanism of NOR degradation was investigated in the photocatalytic system with CQD-CNs/BWO. To better understand the mechanisms of NOR degradation, the intermediates were detected by HPLC-MS/MS and tentative transformation pathways were proposed. The total ion chromatograms were shown in Fig. S7. The details of intermediates including retention time, m/z, and structures were listed in Table S4, and the possible NOR degradation pathways were presented in Fig. 7 . Two main degradation pathways were proposed. The main pathway was cleavage of the piperazine ring by the generated reactive species. Firstly, the NOR was oxidized to Compound I (m/z = 350), and then it produced Compound II (m/z = 322) and III (m/z = 322) via the loss of a –CO group, and the Compound II and III were isomers, which was caused by the generated reactive species attacked the different active sites on Compound I (Jin et al., 2019). The Compound IV (m/z = 294) was generated by further the loss of a –CO group. Compound IV could further transfer to Compound V (m/z = 251) via the functional group of –NH–(CH2)2–NH2 oxidized and decaronylated (Du et al., 2019). And the produced Compound V could be oxidized to Compound VII (m/z = 175) by the ·OH (Huang et al., 2017b). Compound VIII (m/z = 114) was generated via the process of loss of functional groups –NH2 and –CH3 and decarboxylation and hydroxylation (Zhou et al., 2017). In the other pathway, the reactive species oxidation leads to dehalogenation, the decarboxylation, and the piperazine ring cleavage of NOR to form Compound VI (m/z = 277) (Peng et al., 2018). At last, the produced intermediates were expected to be successfully oxidized to generate easily mineralized small molecular organics or inorganic substances by reactive species (Guo et al., 2017).

The intermediates might have more toxicity than NOR; therefore, the toxicities of generated intermediates were evaluated. As shown in Fig. S8 and Fig. S9, the toxicity of the solution changed with the increase of the light period length. The diameter of the inhibition zone of NOR solution (0 min) was approximately 11.05 mm. And, the diameter increased to 11.1 mm at 10 min, indicating that the produced intermediates were more toxic than NOR. Whereas, the diameter of the inhibition zone decreased to 7.95 mm at 20 min, indicating that the toxicity of the solution dropped rapidly. At last, the diameters of the inhibition zone decreased from 11.15 mm to 6.1 mm at 30–120 min. The above results showed that the change of solution toxicity was divided into four stages including enhanced toxicity (0–10 min), decreased toxicity (10–20 min), enhanced toxicity (20–30 min), and decreased toxicity (30–120 min). The results showed that more toxic intermediates were produced in the stages of 0–10, 20–30 min, but the final toxicity of the solution was less than NOR solution and could be almost ignored.

The reusability and stability were important parameters for evaluating the practical application of materials (Sharma et al., 2019b). For evaluating the reusability of CQD-CNs/WBO for photodegradation of NOR, five recycles were performed. As shown in Fig. S10(a), CQD-CNs/WBO exhibited excellent reusability, because its photocatalytic performance still kept at 61.9% compared to the first time at 67.6%. In addition, for determining the stability of CQD-CNs/WBO, XRD and FTIR were hired. After the photocatalytic reaction, the characteristic peaks did not changed significantly, indicating the good stability of CQD-CNs/WBO (Fig. S10(b)). Furthermore, the characteristic functional groups on the surface of CQD-CNs/WBO still existed (Fig. S10(c)). The SEM of CQD-CNs/BWO before and after the photocatalytic experiment was shown in Fig. S11. It could be seen that there was no obvious change in their morphologies. The above results showed that CQD-CNs/WBO not only possessed good reusability but also good stability.

The novel material of CQD-CNs/BWO was successfully prepared via the hydrothermal method. CQDs were attached on the surface of the 2D/2D CNs/BWO structure. Importantly, CQD-CNs/BWO possessed excellent photocatalytic activity compared to CNs, BWO, and CNs/BWO. The reaction rate constant for degradation of NOR with CQD-CNs/BWO was 9 and 1.76 times, respectively, higher than those with pure CNs and BWO. The enhanced photocatalytic activity was attributed to constructed heterojunctions and modified CQDs, which not only improved light utilization but also suppressed electron-hole recombination, and promoted faster interfacial charge transfer. h+ and ·O2 − were the main reactive species to degrade NOR. At last, CQD-CNs/BWO showed excellent reusability and stability. This work provided an illustrative example for removing antibiotics according to practical requirements.
The authors declare no competing financial interests.
